items.title: ⚠️ To all the people who are considering HSBC Demat account route to maintain TRV for Premier Account….. HSBC Demat Account, in its current form, cannot be a substitute for your regular demat account (like Zerodha), because HSBC currently does not offer an associated trading account. 👉 This means that once you transfer your Stocks or ETFs to your HSBC Demat Account, you cannot buy or sell them online like you do with a regular broker. ⏳ Selling a stock or ETF, which usually takes just a few seconds through Zerodha or any other trading platform, can take 5–7 days with HSBC Demat due to the offline process. There are only two ways to transact: 📌 Option 1: Contact your HSBC Relationship Manager (RM) and place an offline buy/sell request. 📌 Option 2: Transfer your Stocks/ETFs back from HSBC Demat to your regular demat account (like Zerodha). For this, you’ll need to fill the physical Delivery Instruction Slip (DIS), courier or hand it over to your RM, who will then initiate the transfer. ⏳ Both processes generally take around 5–7 days to complete. 💡 Earlier, HSBC had a tie-up with ICICI Securities, which allowed customers to buy and sell Stocks/ETFs instantly through an online trading account. That partnership ended last year. HSBC representatives have informed that they are working on bringing back an online trading facility, but there is no confirmed timeline yet. 📈 Most people (including myself) who have chosen the Demat route to maintain the Total Relationship Value (TRV) for the HSBC Premier Account have transferred only that portion of their Stocks/ETF portfolio which they do not intend to sell in the near future and are comfortable keeping locked in an account where selling may take 5–7 days. So, please understand these operational limitations before transferring your investments. Make an informed decision based on your own requirements, and if needed, consult your financial advisor before taking any such step. 📘 Visit SpendWisely Blog: Detailed credit card reviews & guides 👉 https://spendwisely.in Some links may be referral links in the above post. items.title: Despite so many talk about HDFC Diners Club Black Metal (DCBM) Credit Card, its still an underrrated card. The DCBM Card delivers an impressive 5.83% effective reward rate on almost all regular online & offline spends (excluding the usual exclusions like fuel, rent, government payments, wallets, etc.). 💳 Here’s how: 👉 Base Rewards: 3.33% (5 Reward Points per ₹150 spent) 👉 Quarterly Milestone: +2.5% (10,000 Bonus Reward Points on ₹4 lakh quarterly spend) ✅ Total Effective Return: 5.83% on general spending. That’s an exceptional baseline reward rate for a premium credit card across everyday spending categories. On top of it, you get… 👇 🔥 Accelerated Reward Rates: 🏨 33.33% on Hotels, Jockey & PharmEasy ✈️ 16.66% on Flights, Bus Bookings & Myntra 🎁 10% on Gift Vouchers (including useful ones like Swiggy, Uber, etc.) To further increase reward rate, transfer the Reward Points at 1:1 to airline partners and redeem them strategically. ✈️ Flying Blue (Air France/KLM) – Redeem for Air France, KLM, Vietnam Airlines, Oman Air, Etihad, Saudia, Kenya Airways, Virgin Atlantic & more in one single airmile program. ✈️ Finnair Plus (Avios) – Transfer 1:1 to Finnair and then move Avios 1:1 to British Airways, Qatar Airways, Iberia, Aer Lingus. Great for flights on Qatar Airways, British Airways, Cathay Pacific, SriLankan Airlines, Iberia, Aer Lingus, Finnair & more. ✈️ Singapore Airlines KrisFlyer – Excellent for Singapore Airlines flights and Star Alliance redemptions across Asia-Pacific, Australia and beyond. ⚠️Acceptance Issue: Diners Club network’s acceptance issue is not as big as Amex and it has improved a lot in last few years. In fact, it’s almost negligible in metros and online spend. For anyone who spends consistently and knows how to redeem airline miles, DCBM remains one of the strongest all-round premium credit cards available today. 💯 📘 Visit SpendWisely Blog: Detailed credit card reviews & guides 👉 https://spendwisely.in Some links may be referral links in the above post.
